description: Propose recommendations for the seeded electronics-spike anomaly. Inputs are the Anomaly + reference Diagnosis (Path C). PR F established this fixture passes 4-5/5 on the rubric (10/10 pass; one impact-sizing FAIL caught by the BRL-cents bug).

anomaly.impact: order_count for category:electronics rose ~150% in a discrete window. Investigate the root cause.

golden_output.title: Re-engage spike-week electronics buyers with a post-purchase cross-sell campaign
golden_output.rationale: 8,800 customers bought electronics during the mid-December demand surge — likely motivated by a promotional or pre-holiday event. AOV held flat (~$264 USD) while volume was 2.5×, meaning these are genuine buyers who are now post-purchase. Reaching them within 7–14 days with a complementary-product offer (accessories, warranties, home_decor bundles) capitalises on recency while purchase intent is still warm. No other category moved with electronics, so a laser-targeted electronics cross-sell will not cannibalise broad campaigns.
steps: Define an audience segment: customers who placed an 'order' event with category = 'electronics' between 2025-12-08 and 2025-12-15.
Build a broadcast email campaign titled 'Complete your setup — accessories & add-ons for your new electronics'.
Populate the email with product recommendations from complementary categories (cables, cases, smart-home, warranties) pulled from the product catalog.
Schedule the send for 7–10 days post-purchase peak (approx. 2025-12-22) — still inside the holiday gifting window.
Set a control holdout of 10% to measure incremental lift vs organic repeat purchase rate.
Track clicks → orders with UTM parameter utm_campaign=elec-spike-crossell for clean attribution.

estimatedImpact.assumption: Assumes 6–10% conversion of 8,800 spike-week buyers (528–880 orders) at ~$264 USD AOV. Conversion range reflects typical post-purchase email benchmarks for electronics (5–12%).

prerequisites.label: Email channel configured and sending verified
Customer email addresses captured at order time
Complementary product feed / catalog available for dynamic content

golden_output.successMetric: Repeat-purchase rate among spike-week electronics buyers within 30 days. Baseline (organic): ~5%. Target: ≥10% (2× lift), equating to ≥440 additional orders.

golden_output.title: Launch a recurring 'Electronics Demand Spike' trigger scenario to auto-activate next surge
golden_output.rationale: The +150% order-count spike was isolated to electronics and distributed across all major Brazilian states, strongly suggesting a repeatable promotional or seasonal trigger (e.g. Black Friday, Cyber Monday, pre-holiday clearance). Without an automated scenario, the team must manually detect and react each time. A triggered flow that fires when electronics weekly order count exceeds 1.5× the 4-week rolling average will let the merchant capitalise on every future surge automatically — sending real-time acquisition pushes, adjusting ad budgets, or gating vouchers — before the peak subsides.
steps: Create a new automation scenario named 'Electronics Surge Detector'.

Configure the entry trigger: aggregate 'order' events where category = 'electronics', compute a 7-day rolling count, and fire the scenario when that count exceeds 150% of the prior 4-week weekly average.
Branch 1 — New buyers (first electronics purchase): send a same-day email with top-rated electronics + social proof ('X people bought this today').
Branch 2 — Repeat buyers: send a personalised 'Back for more?' email surfacing accessories or upgrades based on prior purchase category.
Add a parallel webhook action to notify the marketing ops Slack channel so paid-media budgets can be manually boosted for electronics keywords.
Set scenario frequency cap: max 1 trigger per customer per 30 days to avoid fatigue.
A/B test subject lines (urgency vs curiosity) on 50/50 split at first activation.

estimatedImpact.assumption: Assumes 1–2 comparable surge events over the next 6 months, each yielding 3,500–7,000 incremental reachable buyers converting at 5–8% at ~$264 USD AOV. Lower bound = 1 surge × 3,500 buyers × 5% × $264; upper = 2 surges × 7,000 × 8% × $264.

prerequisites.label: Real-time order event stream available for scenario trigger evaluation
Slack or webhook integration for ops notification

golden_output.successMetric: Revenue generated per surge event via the scenario vs the Dec-2025 baseline (no automation). Target: ≥$92k incremental revenue at next detected spike, measurable within 7 days of trigger firing.

golden_output.title: A/B test a time-limited electronics voucher to quantify price-sensitivity and defend margin
golden_output.rationale: The surge diagnosis supports a promotional or clearance driver — meaning some portion of the 8,800 buyers may have been price-sensitive deal-seekers rather than brand loyalists. Before the merchant reflexively offers discounts to recover or retain this cohort, an experiment is needed to distinguish price-sensitive buyers (who need a voucher to re-purchase) from intent-driven buyers (who would repurchase anyway). A controlled voucher experiment on 50% of the spike cohort will reveal whether discounting is truly necessary, protecting margin on the 50% that convert organically.
steps: Segment the 8,800 spike-week electronics buyers into two random halves (Group A: voucher, Group B: control — no voucher).
Create a voucher pool: 10% off next electronics purchase, valid for 21 days, single-use, min. order R$200.
Send Group A an email on 2025-12-18: 'Thank you for your purchase — here's 10% off your next electronics order' with the unique voucher code.
Send Group B the same email without the voucher (pure relationship/content message).
Hold experiment for 21 days (voucher validity window); measure repeat-purchase rate and revenue per recipient in both groups.
If voucher lift (Group A conversion − Group B conversion) > 3 percentage points, roll out vouchers to future surge cohorts. If lift < 3 pp, discontinue discounting for this segment.

estimatedImpact.range: $23k – $70k margin protected vs blanket discounting (or +$35k – $92k incremental revenue if voucher lifts conversion)
estimatedImpact.assumption: If voucher is unnecessary (lift < 3 pp): avoiding a 10% discount on ~880 organic repeat orders at $264 AOV saves ~$23k in margin. If voucher works (lift ≥ 3 pp on 8,800 buyers): 3–4% net incremental conversion = 264–352 extra orders × $264 = $70k–$93k revenue.

prerequisites.label: A/B split randomisation available in campaign builder

golden_output.successMetric: Repeat-purchase rate: Group A (voucher) vs Group B (control) at day 21. Baseline control rate assumed ~5%. Decision threshold: proceed with vouchers if Group A rate ≥ 8% (≥3 pp lift); discontinue if below.

scoring_config.notes: Same required fields as 06. PR F's BRL-cents bug (the agent treating price_brl integers as Reais and quoting BRL 131,965 AOV) is visible in the golden's estimatedImpact text — if a prompt change FIXES the bug, the similarity judge should flag it (different impact magnitude = different conclusion).
